domain_true.statement: Tim Berners-Lee invented the World Wide Web in 1989 while working at CERN.
The first web browser was also a web editor, created by Tim Berners-Lee.
HTTP stands for Hypertext Transfer Protocol.
HTML stands for Hypertext Markup Language.
Tim Berners-Lee wrote the first HTTP server software called CERN httpd.
The first website went live on August 6, 1991.
Tim Berners-Lee founded the World Wide Web Consortium known as W3C.
W3C develops open standards to ensure the long-term growth of the Web.
URLs provide a unique address for resources on the World Wide Web.
Tim Berners-Lee proposed the Web through a document titled "Information Management: A Proposal."
The original proposal described a system using hypertext to link documents across networked computers.
Tim Berners-Lee received a knighthood from Queen Elizabeth II in 2004.
The Semantic Web extends the Web by giving data well-defined meaning for machine interpretation.
RDF stands for Resource Description Framework and is a core Semantic Web standard.
Tim Berners-Lee holds the 3Com Founders Chair at MIT's Computer Science and Artificial Intelligence Laboratory.
OWL stands for Web Ontology Language and is used to represent knowledge on the Semantic Web.
SPARQL is a query language designed for retrieving data stored in RDF format.
Tim Berners-Lee received the Turing Award in 2016 for inventing the World Wide Web.
The first web server ran on a NeXT computer at CERN in Geneva, Switzerland.
Linked Data is a method of publishing structured data so it can be interlinked across the Web.
Tim Berners-Lee launched the Solid project to give individuals control over their personal data.
Solid uses decentralized data stores called Pods where users store their own data.
The World Wide Web was designed to be an open and decentralized system from its inception.
HTTP operates as a request-response protocol between clients and servers.
HTML documents are the foundation of content on the World Wide Web.
Tim Berners-Lee has advocated for net neutrality as essential to the open Web.
The Web was made available on a royalty-free basis by CERN in 1993.
URIs provide a standardized way to identify resources on the Web.
Tim Berners-Lee co-founded the Open Data Institute with Nigel Shadbolt in 2012.
The Open Data Institute is based in London and promotes open data practices.
HTTP status code 404 indicates that a requested resource was not found on the server.
HTTPS adds encryption to HTTP using Transport Layer Security.
Tim Berners-Lee appeared in the opening ceremony of the 2012 London Olympic Games.
During the Olympic ceremony, Tim Berners-Lee tweeted "This is for everyone."
The World Wide Web is distinct from the internet, which is the underlying network infrastructure.
HTML uses tags to structure content within web pages.
The first web page described the World Wide Web project itself.
HTTP/1.1 introduced persistent connections to improve performance over HTTP/1.0.
Tim Berners-Lee has been a professor at the University of Oxford.
The Web Foundation was co-founded by Tim Berners-Lee to advance the open Web as a public good.
Data sovereignty refers to the idea that data is subject to the laws of the country where it is stored.
The Semantic Web aims to make internet data machine-readable, not just human-readable.
Tim Berners-Lee has consistently opposed mass government surveillance of web users.
Hypertext allows users to navigate between documents through clickable links.
The concept of hypertext predates the World Wide Web and was coined by Ted Nelson in the 1960s.
HTML was influenced by SGML, the Standard Generalized Markup Language.
HTTP methods include GET, POST, PUT, DELETE, and PATCH.
The GET method requests data from a specified resource without modifying it.
The POST method submits data to a server for processing.
Web browsers interpret HTML to render visual web pages for users.
CSS is used alongside HTML to control the visual presentation of web pages.
JavaScript enables interactive behavior within web pages rendered by browsers.
The Domain Name System translates human-readable domain names into IP addresses.
Tim Berners-Lee has called for a new Contract for the Web to protect online rights.
The Contract for the Web was launched in November 2019 with support from governments and companies.
Web accessibility ensures that people with disabilities can perceive, navigate, and interact with websites.
The Web Content Accessibility Guidelines are published by W3C's Web Accessibility Initiative.
HTTP cookies allow servers to store small pieces of data on a user's browser.
REST is an architectural style for designing networked applications that relies on HTTP.
Tim Berners-Lee did not patent the World Wide Web and made it freely available to all.
The internet uses the TCP/IP protocol suite for data transmission between networks.
A web server is software that responds to HTTP requests from clients.
Apache and Nginx are widely used open-source web server software programs.
The first image uploaded to the Web was a photo of the CERN band Les Horribles Cernettes.
HTML5 introduced native support for audio and video elements without requiring plugins.
The DOCTYPE declaration tells a browser which version of HTML a page is written in.
Semantic HTML uses elements like article, section, and nav to convey meaning about content structure.
Tim Berners-Lee has expressed concern about the concentration of power among a few large web platforms.
Linked Data principles include using URIs to name things and providing useful information at those URIs.
The five-star deployment scheme for open data was proposed by Tim Berners-Lee.
Five-star open data uses linked RDF with connections to other datasets as its highest level.
An ontology in the Semantic Web defines the relationships between concepts in a domain.
CERN is the European Organization for Nuclear Research, located near Geneva, Switzerland.
The internet predates the World Wide Web by approximately two decades.
ARPANET was a precursor to the modern internet and became operational in 1969.
Tim Berners-Lee's original web browser was called WorldWideWeb and ran on the NeXT operating system.
A hyperlink is a reference in a document that a user can follow to reach another document or resource.
The HTTP response code 200 means the request was successful.
The HTTP response code 301 indicates a permanent redirect to a new URL.
The HTTP response code 500 indicates an internal server error.
Web standards promote interoperability so that websites work consistently across different browsers.
The Semantic Web stack includes technologies such as RDF, OWL, SPARQL, and XML.
XML stands for Extensible Markup Language and is used to encode documents in a machine-readable format.
JSON has become a widely used data interchange format on the Web alongside XML.
An API allows different software applications to communicate with each other over the Web.
Tim Berners-Lee received the Order of Merit from Queen Elizabeth II in 2007.
The Web Foundation has published research on the digital gender gap in internet access worldwide.
Data portability allows users to transfer their personal data between different service providers.
Decentralization of data storage reduces single points of failure and control on the Web.
The Solid specification is built on existing Web standards including HTTP, RDF, and WebID.
WebID is a method of identifying users using URIs and their web profiles.
A web crawler is software that systematically browses the Web to index content for search engines.
The robots.txt file tells web crawlers which pages on a site should not be indexed.
HTTPS uses port 443 by default, while HTTP uses port 80 by default.
TLS certificates verify the identity of a website and enable encrypted connections.
The same-origin policy is a security mechanism that restricts how documents from one origin interact with another.
Cross-origin resource sharing allows servers to specify which origins can access their resources.
The Web Payments Working Group at W3C develops standards for online payment processing.
Web components allow developers to create reusable custom HTML elements with encapsulated functionality.
Progressive web applications combine web and native app features to work offline and load quickly.
The WebAssembly standard enables high-performance code execution in web browsers.
HTTP/2 introduced multiplexing, allowing multiple requests and responses over a single TCP connection.
HTTP/3 uses the QUIC transport protocol instead of TCP for improved performance.
Content negotiation in HTTP allows a server to serve different representations of a resource based on client preferences.
The Referer header in HTTP indicates the URL of the page that linked to the requested resource.
Caching in HTTP reduces server load and latency by storing copies of responses for reuse.
The ETag header provides a mechanism for cache validation of web resources.
Tim Berners-Lee has stated that access to the Web should be treated as a basic human right.
The Web Accessibility Initiative was launched by W3C to improve accessibility for people with disabilities.
Internationalized Resource Identifiers extend URIs to support characters from the Universal Character Set.
The World Wide Web Consortium has member organizations from around the globe.
Microdata, RDFa, and JSON-LD are formats for embedding structured data within web pages.
Schema.org provides a shared vocabulary for structured data markup understood by major search engines.
The internet governance ecosystem includes organizations such as ICANN, IETF, and W3C.
ICANN coordinates the global Domain Name System and IP address allocation.
The Internet Engineering Task Force develops and promotes voluntary internet standards including HTTP specifications.
Net neutrality is the principle that internet service providers should treat all data traffic equally.
The digital divide refers to the gap between populations with and without effective access to digital technology.
Tim Berners-Lee has warned that misinformation spreads rapidly on the Web due to algorithmic amplification.
Personal data stores in the Solid framework let users grant and revoke access to their data at any time.

domain_false.statement: Tim Berners-Lee invented the World Wide Web in 1994 at MIT.
HTTP was originally developed by Vint Cerf at Stanford University.
HTML was first standardized by the IEEE in 1988.
The first website ever created was hosted at CERN in 1987.
Tim Berners-Lee sold the World Wide Web technology to Microsoft in 1995.
The original HTTP specification supported only POST and DELETE methods.
HTML was derived directly from SGML's competitor language GML developed at IBM.
The World Wide Web Consortium was founded in 1998 by Tim Berners-Lee and Marc Andreessen.
HTTP/2 was released in 2020 as a replacement for HTTP/1.1.
The first web browser was called Mosaic and was built by Tim Berners-Lee.
Tim Berners-Lee patented the concept of hyperlinks in 1990.
The semantic web was first proposed by Tim Berners-Lee in 2005 at a Google conference.
HTTPS was invented by Netscape in 1999 to secure online banking transactions.
The original HTML specification included support for JavaScript embedding.
Tim Berners-Lee developed the World Wide Web while working at Bell Labs.
HTTP status code 404 was named after a room number at Stanford where the first server was housed.
The Domain Name System was created as part of the original World Wide Web proposal.
RDF was developed by Microsoft as a proprietary data format for Internet Explorer.
Tim Berners-Lee currently serves as CEO of the Internet Corporation for Assigned Names and Numbers.
The first web server ran on a Windows NT operating system.
HTML5 was finalized and released as a W3C Recommendation in 2010.
The World Wide Web was originally designed as a commercial e-commerce platform.
Tim Berners-Lee received the Nobel Prize in Physics for inventing the Web.
HTTP cookies were part of the original HTTP/1.0 specification authored by Berners-Lee.
The semantic web initiative was abandoned by the W3C in 2015.
URI stands for Universal Resource Indicator and was coined by Robert Cailliau.
The first website displayed a photograph of Tim Berners-Lee and his team.
CSS was included in the original HTML specification as an inline styling mechanism.
Tim Berners-Lee founded the Internet Engineering Task Force in 1992.
The original web proposal at CERN was immediately approved and fully funded.
OWL, the Web Ontology Language, was developed by Oracle for database management.
HTTP/3 uses TCP as its underlying transport protocol.
Tim Berners-Lee wrote the first web browser using the Java programming language.
The World Wide Web and the internet are the same thing, created simultaneously.
SPARQL was developed by Amazon as a query language for cloud databases.
The first web page was accessed by over one million users within its first month.
Tim Berners-Lee's original proposal was titled "Information Management: A Solution."
HTML was the first markup language ever created for electronic documents.
The W3C is headquartered exclusively in Silicon Valley, California.
Linked Data principles were first articulated by James Hendler in 2001.
The original WorldWideWeb browser could only display plain text without any formatting.
HTTP was designed from the start to maintain persistent stateful connections.
Tim Berners-Lee launched the Solid project in partnership with Facebook in 2016.
The first URL ever used was www.google.com.
XML was created as a direct replacement for HTML by the W3C in 1995.
The internet was invented by Tim Berners-Lee in 1989 alongside the World Wide Web.
Data sovereignty refers exclusively to government ownership of all citizen data.
The W3C has legal authority to enforce web standards compliance on all websites.
Tim Berners-Lee was knighted by the Queen of England in 2010.
The original HTTP protocol included built-in encryption for all data transfers.
Microformats were developed by the W3C as the official replacement for RDF.
The semantic web stack does not include any ontology languages.
Tim Berners-Lee developed TCP/IP as a prerequisite for the World Wide Web.
The Web Accessibility Initiative was started by Google in 2005.
HTML forms were not introduced until HTML version 4.0 in 1999.
The original web proposal at CERN was written jointly by Tim Berners-Lee and Linus Torvalds.
REST was an architectural style defined by Tim Berners-Lee in 1995.
The W3C merged with ICANN in 2018 to form a single internet governance body.
JSON-LD was created by Apple as a proprietary data interchange format.
The first web browser supported video playback and streaming audio natively.
HTTP PUT and DELETE methods were only added in the HTTP/2 specification.
Tim Berners-Lee opposed making the World Wide Web free and open to the public.
The semantic web is designed to be readable only by humans, not machines.
XHTML was developed by Netscape as a competitor to the W3C's HTML standard.
The Web Foundation was established by Bill Gates in 2008.
Tim Berners-Lee's original NeXT computer used to build the Web was destroyed in 1995.
Internet governance is managed exclusively by the United States federal government.
The robots.txt standard was created by the W3C as an official web specification.
RDFa was developed exclusively for use in mobile applications.
Tim Berners-Lee proposed the World Wide Web as a replacement for email.
The first web server software was called Internet Information Services.
HTML tags were originally enclosed in square brackets rather than angle brackets.
The W3C charges licensing fees for the use of all web standards it publishes.
WebSocket protocol was part of the original HTTP/1.0 specification.
Tim Berners-Lee co-founded Google with Larry Page in 1998.
The semantic web requires all websites to use artificial intelligence.
ICANN was founded by Tim Berners-Lee to manage domain name allocation.
The original WorldWideWeb browser was developed for Microsoft DOS.
HTTP content negotiation was first introduced in HTTP/3.
The Web Content Accessibility Guidelines were first published in 2012.
Tim Berners-Lee developed the PNG image format for use on the early Web.
The multistakeholder model of internet governance gives veto power to national governments.
SVG was developed by Adobe as a proprietary format before being donated to the W3C.
The first website outside of CERN was launched in Japan in 1990.
Berners-Lee's Solid project aims to centralize all user data under W3C control.
The original HTML had twenty-two different heading levels.
HTTP response headers were not part of the protocol until version 2.0.
The Web was originally accessible only through command-line interfaces until 1998.
Tim Berners-Lee served as the chief technology officer of Amazon from 2001 to 2005.
The semantic web vision has been fully realized and implemented across all major websites.
DNS over HTTPS was invented by Tim Berners-Lee in 2012.
The W3C operates as a for-profit corporation based in Geneva, Switzerland.
Net neutrality was a concept first introduced by Tim Berners-Lee in his 1989 proposal.
The original HTTP specification defined twelve distinct request methods.
Tim Berners-Lee developed RSS as the first syndication format for the Web.
The Web Payments Working Group at W3C was established to create Bitcoin standards.
HTML entities were borrowed directly from the LaTeX typesetting system.
The first graphical web browser was developed by Tim Berners-Lee at CERN in 1993.
Data sovereignty laws originated in the United States with the Digital Millennium Copyright Act.
The semantic web relies exclusively on XML and cannot use any other data format.
WebAssembly was created by the W3C as a replacement for HTML.
Tim Berners-Lee's original web proposal included detailed specifications for search engines.
The Internet Governance Forum was created by the W3C in 2010.
HTTP/1.1 was published in 2005 as a major revision of the original protocol.
The W3C's Patent Policy requires all members to patent their contributions.
Tim Berners-Lee developed the concept of cloud computing in 1996.
The original web architecture required all pages to be written in English.
RDFS was developed by Yahoo as a schema language for advertising data.
The General Data Protection Regulation was drafted by the W3C.
Tim Berners-Lee opposed the development of CSS, preferring inline styling only.
The first transatlantic web connection was established in 1996 between MIT and Oxford.
Semantic web technologies are incompatible with existing relational databases.
The Web of Things initiative was started by Samsung independently of the W3C.
Tim Berners-Lee's Contract for the Web was published in 2015.
HTTP/1.0 supported multiplexing of multiple requests over a single connection.
The W3C has the authority to shut down websites that violate web standards.
Tim Berners-Lee invented the concept of open-source software licensing.
The original HTML specification included native support for database queries.
Internet governance was formalized at the World Summit on the Information Society in 2001.
The Linked Open Data movement was initiated by Google's Knowledge Graph team.
